provenance.title: Reinforcement Learning for Trade Execution with Market and Limit Orders
affiliations: Department of Mathematics, ETH Zurich, Switzerland
provenance.abstract: We introduce a novel reinforcement learning framework for optimal trade execution in a limit order book. We formulate the trade execution problem as a dynamic allocation task whose objective is the optimal placement of market and limit orders to maximize expected revenue. By modeling market and limit order allocations with multivariate logistic-normal distributions, the framework enables efficient training of the reinforcement learning algorithm. Numerical experiments show that the proposed method outperforms traditional benchmark strategies in simulated limit order book environments.
key_claims: Novel actor-critic RL algorithm using the logistic-normal distribution to model random allocations on the simplex, with a new closed-form policy gradient expression applicable to any dynamic allocation task
General state/action space formulation for trade execution that tracks full limit order book state including queue positions of the algorithm's own orders
Detailed empirical analysis in simulated markets with noise, tactical, and strategic traders that generate direct and indirect market impact reacting to the algorithm's own trades
Logistic-normal (LN) policy outperforms heuristic benchmarks (submit-and-leave, TWAP) and a Dirichlet-distribution (DR) RL benchmark across most tested market/position configurations
Ablation studies show order book volume features are the most important inputs, followed by order-flow/mid-price-drift features, then own order level/queue-position features

modules.name: PolicyNetwork
modules.notes: Maps market+private state s to mean vector mu_theta in R^K of the underlying multivariate normal distribution used to construct the logistic-normal policy. Output layer bias initialized to (-1,...,-1); other output weights initialized with orthogonal init, gain 1e-5.

modules.name: ValueNetwork
modules.notes: Critic network V_phi(s) estimating expected future normalized reward, used to compute advantage estimates via Monte Carlo return minus baseline (GAE with lambda=1).

modules.name: LogisticNormalTransform
modules.notes: Maps x in R^K sampled from N(mu_theta, Sigma) to action a in simplex S^K via a_k = exp(x_k)/(1+sum exp(x_l)) for k<K and a_K = 1/(1+sum exp(x_l)). Inverse: x_k = log(a_k/a_K).

modules.name: DirichletPolicyNetwork
modules.notes: Benchmark algorithm (DR): outputs concentration parameters alpha of a Dirichlet distribution on the simplex. Softplus keeps outputs positive; bias initialized so alpha ~ (1,...,1,10).

modules.name: LimitOrderBookSimulator
modules.notes: Discrete-price limit order book with best bid/ask, D=30 visible price levels per side, queues of resting limit orders per level, matched FIFO within a level.

modules.name: NoiseTraderAgents
modules.notes: Submit market/limit/cancellation orders via independent Poisson processes, sizes from shifted half-normal distribution (delta=2, capped at 5 std devs), following Abergel and Jedidi (2013) intensities with cancellation intensities scaled x100.

modules.name: TacticalTraderAgents
modules.notes: Order arrival intensities scale with positive/negative parts of weighted volume imbalance I = (V_b - V_a)/(V_b + V_a), weighted with exponential damping e^{-c(k-1)} over levels.

modules.name: StrategicTraderAgent
modules.notes: Randomly buys or sells (p=1/2) a large position; sends market order of size 1 every 3s and limit order of size 2 at best ask every 3s, from t=-dt to T.

differences.variance: learned diagonal log-variance parameter instead of scheduled

mathematical_spec.name: Advantage estimate (Monte Carlo return minus baseline, GAE lambda=1)
Weighted volume imbalance (tactical traders)
mathematical_spec.latex: I = \frac{V^b - V^a}{V^b + V^a},\quad V^b=\sum_{k=1}^D v^{b,k}e^{-c(k-1)},\ V^a=\sum_{k=1}^D v^{a,k}e^{-c(k-1)}

tensor_semantics.name: state_market
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [p^b, p^a, v^b_1..K-1, v^a_1..K-1, dM, dL, dC, dp]

tensor_semantics.name: order_book_volumes
tensor_semantics.shape_notation: [2, D] (bid, ask) x D price levels

training_pipeline.curriculum: Variance (exploration) of the logistic-normal policy is annealed linearly from sigma_init=1.0 to sigma_final=0.1 over H=400 gradient steps (Eq. 12); this progressively shifts training from exploration to exploitation.

datasets.name: Simulated market: Noise traders only
datasets.split: test (10,000 simulations, different random seed than training)

datasets.name: Simulated market: Noise & Tactical & Strategic traders

metrics: expected normalized reward (implementation shortfall, ticks per lot)
baselines: Submit and Leave (SL) — post entire position as a single limit order at t=0
Time-Weighted Average Price (TWAP) — split position into N equal blocks, one limit order per step
evaluation_protocol.compute: AMD EPYC 7763 64-core CPU (128 threads), single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU (24GB VRAM), CUDA 12.8, driver 570.144; training times ~1.2-2 hours per configuration (Table 7)
special_conditions: Deterministic policy used at evaluation time: LN uses h(mu_theta); DR uses Dirichlet mean E[a]=alpha/sum(alpha)
Evaluation uses a different random seed for market simulation than training to avoid overfitting to training simulations
10,000 Monte Carlo simulations used to compute all reported expected values and standard deviations

implementation_assumptions.assumption: beta1/beta2 for Adam optimizer use PyTorch defaults (0.9, 0.999) since not explicitly stated
implementation_assumptions.basis: literature default / common practice, paper only cites Kingma & Ba (2017) and specifies lr=0.0005

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Neural network framework is PyTorch, based on CleanRL PPO codebase reference
implementation_assumptions.basis: paper states implementation is 'based on the implementation of PPO by Huang et al. (2022)' whose CleanRL repo is PyTorch-based

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Reward is computed at each of the N=10 decision steps as net cash flow from market/limit fills within (t_n, t_{n+1}], normalized by M and initial bid price, matching Eq. 3
Order cancellation priority within the algorithm's own orders always cancels the order with the worst (highest) queue position first when reallocating, matching the paper's stated rule
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated in Section 3.2 and illustrated in Figure 1

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Rounding of a(t)*M(t) to integers is done sequentially level-by-level (a_0, a_1, ..., a_{K-1}) ensuring the running sum never exceeds M(t)
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated in Section 3.2, exact rounding algorithm (e.g., largest remainder vs. sequential floor) not fully specified

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Vectorized environment uses 128 parallel CPU-simulated markets each collecting 10 trajectories per training iteration, giving tau=1280 trajectories total
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated in Appendix B.1 (Table 5, 'Vectorized sample collection')

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Orthogonal weight initialization gain is 0.01 for all layers except the final policy layer (gain 1e-5) and value network follows the same orthogonal scheme; exact per-layer gains for the value network are not explicitly restated
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated for policy network in Appendix B.1; value network initialization scheme is implied to be the same but not explicitly re-confirmed

implementation_assumptions.assumption: K=6 (simplex dimension: market order + 5 limit-order tick levels + 1 no-order action) used for all three simulated markets
Random seeds for reproducibility are not explicitly given in the paper; a fixed seed convention (e.g., seed=0 for training, seed=1 for eval) is assumed for the reference implementation
implementation_assumptions.basis: not stated in paper, standard reproducibility practice
alternatives: seed reported in released code at https://github.com/moritzweiss/rlte

ambiguities.description: The exact tie-breaking/rounding procedure converting continuous simplex allocations a(t)*M(t) into integer lot allocations is described only at a high level ('round sequentially, assuring total allocated orders do not exceed M(t)'); the precise algorithm (e.g. floor vs. round, order of tie-breaking among equal fractional parts) is not fully specified.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Sequential floor-then-remainder-distribution scheme processing levels 0..K-1 in order
alternatives: stochastic rounding proportional to fractional part

ambiguities.description: Weight initialization details (orthogonal init with gain 0.01 for non-final layers, 1e-5 for final policy layer) are stated for the policy network; it is not fully explicit whether the same gains apply identically to the value network's hidden and output layers.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Value network uses orthogonal init with gain 0.01 for all layers including output
alternatives: Different gain for value output layer
standard PyTorch default init for value net

ambiguities.description: The paper uses lambda_GAE=1 (i.e., full Monte-Carlo return minus baseline) as the primary advantage estimator, with lambda=0.95 tested only in a small-trajectory-count ablation (Appendix C.1) using tau=320 rather than the main tau=1280 setting; it's ambiguous whether other lambda values were tried in the main tau=1280 configuration.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Main results (Table 1) use lambda_GAE=1 with tau=1280

ambiguities.description: Order sizes for noise/tactical traders follow 1 + delta*|Z| with Z standard normal, capped at 5 std devs and rounded to nearest integer; the exact rounding convention (round-half-up vs. round-half-to-even) at the cap boundary is not specified.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Standard round-half-up to nearest integer, capped at floor(1+5*delta)
